Why it matters: Core layer for identifying job clusters and employment centers
Questions it helps answer: Where are jobs concentrated? Are jobs close to where people live?
Likely source: LEHD/LODES, state labor data, local economic development data

What it shows: Type of jobs in each location
Why it matters: Not all jobs are interchangeable; retail, office, industrial, and healthcare jobs function differently
Questions it helps answer: What kinds of jobs are in each area? Are certain industries concentrated in specific parts of the city?
Likely source: LEHD, County Business Patterns, InfoGroup/Data Axle, local business license data

What it shows: Locations of low-, medium-, and high-wage jobs
Why it matters: Essential because mismatch is often about wages, not just jobs
Questions it helps answer: Are low-wage workers living near low-wage jobs? Where are high-wage jobs concentrated?

Why it matters: Helps identify commuting destinations and corridor needs
Questions it helps answer: What are the main job centers? Are they accessible to nearby residents?
Likely source: Local employer datasets, chambers, economic development agencies

What it shows: Jobs per acre or square mile
Why it matters: Helps compare concentration of employment across places
Questions it helps answer: Which areas are truly job-rich? Are job-rich places supported by housing nearby?
Likely source: Derived from employment + parcel/land area data

Why it matters: Basic spatial layer for comparing housing with job patterns
Questions it helps answer: Where is housing located relative to jobs?
Likely source: Parcel data, assessor, local land use data

Why it matters: Housing type influences affordability and worker access
Questions it helps answer: Are there housing types near job centers that match workforce needs?
Likely source: Assessor, parcel data, local planning data, ACS

What it shows: Units per acre or square mile
Why it matters: Useful for measuring jobs-housing balance
Questions it helps answer: Are areas with many jobs also providing enough housing?

What it shows: Rent, home values, or sale prices
Why it matters: Mismatch is often about whether workers can afford nearby housing
Questions it helps answer: Can workers afford to live near their jobs?
Likely source: ACS, Zillow-style datasets, local housing studies

Why it matters: Shows whether lower-income households have access to jobs
Questions it helps answer: Is affordable housing located near employment centers?
Likely source: HUD, local housing authority, state housing finance agency

Why it matters: Important where subsidized housing is limited
Questions it helps answer: Where is lower-cost housing without subsidy located?

GIS layer: New housing permits / recent housing development
What it shows: Where new housing is being added
Why it matters: Shows whether growth is helping or worsening mismatch
Questions it helps answer: Is new housing being built near jobs? What type of housing is being added?

Why it matters: Needed to compare jobs by workplace vs workers by residence
Questions it helps answer: Are residents living near their jobs, or commuting elsewhere?

Why it matters: Helps determine whether local wages align with local housing costs
Questions it helps answer: Are local households able to afford housing near available jobs?

Why it matters: Shows whether workforce supply aligns with local job types
Questions it helps answer: Do nearby residents match the jobs available in the area?

GIS layer: Workers by income bracket / wage bracket
Why it matters: Helps segment mismatch by income group
Questions it helps answer: Which income groups face the greatest housing or commute burden?

Why it matters: Important for transit-based job access analysis
Questions it helps answer: Are job centers reachable for residents without cars?

Why it matters: Helpful context for both service and transportation analysis
Questions it helps answer: Are jobs located near population concentrations?

Why it matters: Required for drive-time accessibility analysis
Questions it helps answer: How easily can workers reach job centers by car?

Why it matters: Critical for understanding non-auto access to jobs
Questions it helps answer: Which job centers are accessible by transit today?

Why it matters: Accessibility depends on service quality, not just route presence
Questions it helps answer: Are jobs realistically reachable by transit, or only technically reachable?

Why it matters: Helps assess first/last-mile access to jobs and transit
Questions it helps answer: Can people safely walk to transit or nearby jobs?

Why it matters: Useful for short commute analysis
Questions it helps answer: Are nearby jobs reachable by bike from housing areas?

What it shows: Where workers live and where they work
Why it matters: Reveals commuting patterns and dependence on inbound/outbound workers
Questions it helps answer: Are jobs serving local residents or commuters from elsewhere?

Why it matters: Explains structural causes of mismatch
Questions it helps answer: Do zoning rules allow housing near jobs or mixed-use development?

Why it matters: Helps test whether scenarios reduce or worsen mismatch
Questions it helps answer: Will future land use improve jobs-housing balance?

Why it matters: Useful for communication and policy targeting
Questions it helps answer: Which districts have the strongest mismatch?

Why it matters: Often more meaningful than housing cost alone
Questions it helps answer: Is an area truly affordable once commuting costs are included?
Likely source: CNT H+T data, ACS + travel data

GIS layer: Median wage of jobs by geography
Why it matters: Strong summary metric for job quality
Questions it helps answer: What is the earning potential of jobs in each part of the city?

What it shows: Balance between employment and housing supply
Why it matters: Classic indicator for mismatch
Questions it helps answer: Which areas have many jobs but too little housing, or vice versa?

Why it matters: More useful than a single overall ratio
Questions it helps answer: Are there enough housing options for low-, medium-, and high-wage workers near corresponding jobs?

What it shows: Areas reachable in 15, 30, 45 minutes by mode
Why it matters: One of the strongest ways to show mismatch
Questions it helps answer: How many jobs can residents reach within a reasonable commute?

What it shows: Whether local wages can support nearby housing costs
Why it matters: One of the most useful mismatch measures
Questions it helps answer: Can someone earning local wages afford housing within a reasonable distance of work?
Likely source: Derived from wage + housing cost data
